quintus_poppaedius_convo_1_n01.npcDialogue: You're back. The roads between Rome and our camp are killing grounds now — the fact that you made it here tells me either you're brave or you're desperate. The Lex Julia has passed — citizenship for Italian communities that lay down their arms. Some of my allies are accepting. The Samnites refuse. The Marsi are split. I have to decide in the next three days whether to take Rome's offer or keep fighting. Convince me.

options.playerDialogue: Take the offer. It's not everything you wanted, but it's citizenship — the thing your people have bled for. Don't let perfection destroy progress.
Keep fighting. The Lex Julia is a trap — they'll revoke it the moment you disarm. You have leverage now. Don't surrender it.
Accept publicly but maintain your army privately. Take the citizenship and keep the weapons until you're sure they'll honor it.

quintus_poppaedius_convo_1_n02.npcDialogue: Progress. You Romans love that word. My father was promised progress. My grandfather was promised progress. Each generation, the promise gets a little shinier and the delivery gets a little slower. But you may be right — the Lex Julia is more than any previous generation received. The question is whether I can sell it to my own people. The Samnites will call me a traitor.

responses.playerDialogue: Let the Samnites fight alone. A treaty honored by both sides is worth more than a war won by neither.
You won't be a traitor — you'll be the man who got his people what they deserved without destroying them in the process.
Tell the Samnites this: the Marsi fight for citizenship, not for independence. You got citizenship. The fight is over.
Frame it as a strategic withdrawal, not a surrender. The Marsi accept peace now and build political power inside the Republic.

fallbackResponse.playerDialogue: I can't tell you what to do. But I can tell you what I've seen in Rome — the will to fight you is fading.

quintus_poppaedius_convo_1_n03.npcDialogue: A Roman telling me to keep fighting Rome. Either you're the most honest man I've met or the most devious. The Samnites would agree with you — they want Italia, not citizenship. But the Marsi? My people are tired. They've buried their sons. They want to go home. I can ask them to die for freedom but I can't ask them to die for strategy.

options.playerDialogue: Then find a middle path. What would satisfy the Marsi without betraying the Samnites?

quintus_poppaedius_convo_1_n04.npcDialogue: Accept and keep the weapons. You think like a Marsic, not a Roman. Yes — that's what I'm considering. The legions are scattered between the Italian front and Sulla's march on Rome. If we accept citizenship now, Rome can't afford to disarm us while fighting itself. By the time they turn their attention back to us, we'll be citizens with veteran armies. Legal ones.

options.playerDialogue: Then the question is how to make the acceptance look genuine enough.

quintus_poppaedius_convo_1_n05.npcDialogue: A middle path. Let me think about what that looks like.

quintus_poppaedius_convo_1_n06.npcDialogue: Here's my proposal. The Marsi accept the Lex Julia — publicly, with ceremony. In exchange, I need Rome to guarantee three things: the Lex Varia prosecutions stop, Marsic veterans receive the same land grants as Roman ones, and the tribal enrollment happens immediately — not in ten years when convenient. Can you carry those terms?

options.playerDialogue: I'll carry them to Sulpicius. He has the legislative power to make it happen.
I'll carry them to whoever is in power when I get back to Rome. Right now, that's an open question.

quintus_poppaedius_convo_1_n07.npcDialogue: You've earned my trust, Roman. That's not a thing I say lightly. Here's what I'm going to do — I'm going to accept the Lex Julia for the Marsi, and I'm going to name you as the Roman guarantor of the peace. That means if Rome breaks its word, you share the dishonor. Are you willing to put your name on that?

options.playerDialogue: My name is on it. If Rome breaks this peace, I'll answer for it personally.
I'll guarantee it in private, but I can't put my name on a public document. Not with the proscriptions happening.

quintus_poppaedius_convo_1_n08.npcDialogue: An open question. Rome doesn't even know who rules it anymore. Fine — I'll make my own decision without Roman input, the way I should have from the start. Go home. Tell whoever sits in the consul's chair that the Marsi decide their own fate. We always have.

quintus_poppaedius_convo_1_n09.npcDialogue: Then it's done. The Marsic war ends — not with a Roman triumph, but with a handshake between two men who chose peace over glory. My people will remember your name. In our language, that's the highest honor we give to an outsider. Go safely, Roman. And keep your word.

quintus_poppaedius_convo_1_n10.npcDialogue: Private guarantees. That's what Rome has always offered — whispered promises that disappear in daylight. I'll take what I can get. The Marsi will accept the Lex Julia, but we'll keep our swords close. Tell Rome we're peaceful. Don't tell them we're trusting.

deltas.reason: Named guarantor of the Marsic peace
The populares claim the peace as a reform victory
Conservative senators view the peace terms as too generous
Chose peace over war at personal risk
The Marsi accept the Lex Julia with reservations
Negotiated a deal without fully committing to either side
Poppaedius no longer trusts Roman intermediaries

effects.reason: The failed negotiation costs you credibility and time
